text: Imagine a really famous, uh, aging king.
Right. A man at the very end of his life.
Exactly. He has lived a life of extreme highs and, well, really devastating lows.
He started out as just a forgotten shepherd boy.
Yeah, a boy who became a giant slayer.
Then he lived as a runaway, hiding in cold, dark mountain caves.
And eventually he rose up to become the ruler of a great nation.
But now, his long life is coming to a close.
He is taking his final breaths, and he sings one last beautiful, prophetic song.
It is a moment of deep, uh, deep quiet.
A moment of spiritual reflection.
But in here is the wild part.
When you look at the ancient record, right next to that beautiful, delicate poetry,
you find something totally different.
You really do. It is almost a shock.
You find these gritty, unbelievably intense, muddy and bloody stories
of his elite ancient military unit, his special forces.
The contrast is jarring.
But it is completely intentional.
You have the peaceful words of a dying king placed right next to stories
of men fighting wild animals in the snow.
Men standing alone against entire armies.
You have the spiritual vision of the leader on one side.
And on the other side, you have the raw, physical reality
of the people who actually built his kingdom.
Welcome to today's Deep Dive.
We are so happy to have you here with us.
We really are.

We are going to focus entirely, and I mean exclusively,
on one single fascinating chapter of ancient text.

We want to look at the brilliant way it was written

we are going to use simple,
everyday English.

not complicated vocabulary.

to understand a broad timeline.

when you look very closely at just one chapter,
you uncover an entire hidden world inside it.

through a few specific lenses today
to really open up that hidden world.

That keeps the story very direct for you.
That helps a lot.

we are using the commentary of Ellen G. White.

the minds,
and even the moral
failures of these ancient leaders.

of a modern scholar named Robert Alter.

His work is so important,
especially his book, The Art of Biblical Narrative.

No fluff.

It was meant to carry heavy meaning.

one alters eye for literature with the historical and psychological lenses.
Chapter 23 changes.
It goes from a simple list of old events into a really deep study of human nature.
Structurally, this chapter is very unique.
Yeah, it is split into two completely different halves.
The first part covers the first seven verses.
That is the beautiful, quiet piece of poetry you mentioned earlier.
These are written down as David's last words.
And then right after that quiet song, the second part of the chapter begins.
It is action-packed.
It is highly cinematic.
It is almost an overwhelming list of his greatest warriors and their crazy military victories.
We go straight from playing the harp to swinging the sword.
Before we look at the text itself, let's just think about the whole idea of last words.
It is a powerful concept.
When someone reaches the very end of their life, the things they choose to say carry
so much weight.
They don't care about daily politics anymore.
Or trivial things.
They're trying to boil down their whole existence.
All their failures.
All their successes.
They want to leave one final message for the next generation.
I want you to think about that for yourself as you listen today.
Just imagine you were at the end of your life.
What would you want your own last words to be?
That is a great question.
What is the final message, the core truth, that you would want to leave behind for your
family or for the world?
Keep that in the back of your mind as we see what King David chose to say.
Let us look at how those final words actually work.
The opening of the chapter sets a very specific mood for everything that follows.
Using the Bible in basic English, the opening verses are really striking.
It says, these are the last words of David.
David, the son of Jesse, says, the man who was lifted up on high, the man on whom the
God of Jacob put the holy oil, the loved one of Israel's songs, says.
The language here is incredibly careful.
The Logos Bible commentary points out a detail in the original Hebrew text that changes everything
about this passage.
What is that detail?
In Hebrew.
In Hebrew, there is a very specific word used here for the word says.
That word is Neom.
Let's unpack that.
Why is Neom so important?
From a language standpoint, this is not the word you use for normal everyday talking.
Not at all.
You wouldn't use Neom to say, hey, the king says it is time for dinner.
Right.
It is exactly the opposite.
Neom is almost only used in these ancient texts for a direct divine message.
Like a message straight from God.
Yes.
An oracle.
Like the ancient prophets.
And they deliver a message directly from heaven, they use this specific word.
It carries that much authority.
In fact, when false prophets wanted to trick people, when they wanted to manipulate the
public into thinking they had a real message from God, they would use this exact word to
make their lies sound official.
Wow.
So by using Neom here, the text is making a massive claim.
It elevates this from just a simple memory.
David is not just an old man sitting in a chair giving his personal opinions about life.
Or giving general political advice.
Or giving advice to his son.
Right.
He is delivering a message that he believes is coming directly from a higher power.
Verse 2 actually spells this out very clearly.
David says, The Spirit of the Lord had voice through me.
His word was on my tongue.
That changes how you as the listener have to receive the information.
It demands your absolute attention.
The divine spirit is speaking through him.
That gives supreme importance to the pictures he uses next to describe leadership.
And the imagery he uses is absolutely beautiful.
He doesn't use military words to describe what makes a good leader.
Instead, he uses simple nature metaphors that everyone can understand.
He says that when a king rules in the fear of God, it is as the light of the morning
when the sun comes up, a morning without clouds, making young grass come to life from the earth.
We really have to place ourselves back in an ancient farming culture to fully appreciate
this.
Because today, if it rains, we just grab an umbrella.
And if the sun shines, we put on sunglasses.
It is not a big deal.
But for the people listening to this song thousands of years ago, the weather was quite
literally a matter of life and death.
Their survival depended entirely on the harvest.
If the crops failed, they starved.
So a morning without clouds, with the bright sun shining on fresh young grass after a rainstorm,
that paints a picture of a world that is thriving.
It brings life.
It brings growth.
The sun gives the energy needed for the crops.
The crops to push through the dirt.
And a good leader, a just ruler, functions the exact same way for their people.
They create an environment where the community can grow, where people can thrive and feel
the warmth of safety and justice.
Now contrast that with the picture used right after to describe a bad leader.
The text says that evil men are like thorns that are thrown away because they cannot be
touched with the bare hand.
Thorns do not produce fruit.
No, they don't provide shade or food either.
Their only job is to cause pain.
They tear your clothes.
They draw blood.
They choke out the good plants around them.
That is a brilliant, simple way to judge any person in a position of power.
You can ask a very simple question.
Does this person act like the morning sun, helping the people around them grow and thrive?
Or do they act like thorns?
Causing pain, creating barriers, and choking the life out of their community.
It is a really deep standard for leadership.
But it does introduce a big historical tension here.
What do you mean?
Well, anyone who knows the history of King David.
Knows that he himself was not always the perfect sunshine and grass kind of leader.
Oh, absolutely not.
He had moments where he was definitely the thorns.
This is where the commentary from Ellen G. White provides such important context.
Her analysis in her book Patriarchs and Prophets does not hide the dark realities of David's
life.
She makes it clear that his life was deeply flawed.
He had a great, terrible fall.
He abused his power.
He took another man's wife.
And then he arranged a murder to cover it up.
His sins were public.
They were devastating.
And they brought terrible conflict and grief to his family and to his whole nation.
So recognizing those massive failures makes this final song so much more complex.
It really does.
You have to ask, how can a man who committed such terrible abuses of power be the one singing
this perfect song about pure leadership?
Ellen White explains that the story of David is not a story about human perfection.
Right.
It is a story about the reality of grace.
And the true depth of saying, you are sorry.
True repentance.
When David was confronted with his terrible actions, he did not act like a normal ancient
king.
A normal tyrant back then would have just killed the person who accused him.
Exactly.
But David did not make excuses.
He did not blame his situation.
He broke down and repented from the very bottom of his heart.
His sadness was totally real.
White's look into David's mind shows a man whose faith was strong, even when his actions
were bad.
Because he understood the depths of his own failure.
And because he understood the depths of his own failure.
He also understood the absolute need for God's mercy.
And this brings us to the core of what his last words actually mean.
This song represents David's ultimate trust in God's eternal agreement.
What the text calls the everlasting covenant.
David says in verse 5 that God made this covenant with him.
David's song proves that no matter how far a person falls, no matter how many terrible
mistakes they make, grace and mercy can reach down, forgive them, and lift them back up.
It is the song of a forgiven man.
Look at how he introduces himself.
He introduces himself in the very beginning.
He calls himself the son of Jesse, who was lifted up on high.
He doesn't say he climbed to the top because he was so strong or so good.
He admits where he came from.
He was just a shepherd, Jesse's boy.
God lifted him up.
And more importantly, God forgave him and held him up when he fell.
That is an incredibly powerful lesson for you, the listener.
It makes you think about how we view our own lives and our own mistakes.
Human nature makes us want to define ourselves by our worst moments.
We let ourselves be defined by our worst moments.
We let our failures become our identity.
But David's last words refuse to do that.
He defines his life by God's forgiveness and God's promise, not by his own darkest days.
It serves as a lasting proof that your failures do not have to be your final words.
The promise of grace is stronger than human mistakes.
So we have this beautiful, quiet picture of an old king singing about sunlight, grass,
